content:
# Exodus Chapter 12 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38v-40r (PDF pp. 73-75); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 52-53 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_012_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_003.jpg`

---

## Institution of the Passover (vv. 1-14)

**12:1** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying: [fn1]

**12:2** "This month shall be for you the head of months; it shall be for you the first of the months of the year. [fn2]

**12:3** Speak to all the congregation of Israel, saying: 'On the tenth of this month, let them take for themselves, each man, a lamb for a father's house, a lamb for a house. [fn3]

**12:4** And if the household is too small for a lamb, then he and his neighbor near to his house shall take according to the number of persons; each man according to his eating you shall reckon for the lamb.

**12:5** A lamb, unblemished, a male, a year old -- it shall be for you. From the sheep or from the goats you shall take it. [fn4]

**12:6** And it shall be for you to keep until the fourteenth day of this month, and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall slaughter it between the evenings. [fn5]

**12:7** And they shall take from the blood and put it on the two doorposts and on the lintel, on the houses in which they eat it. [fn6]

**12:8** And they shall eat the flesh on that night, roasted with fire, and unleavened bread -- with bitter herbs they shall eat it.

**12:9** Do not eat from it raw, or boiled -- boiled in water; but roasted with fire -- its head with its legs and with its inner parts. [fn7]

**12:10** And you shall not leave any of it until morning, and what remains of it until morning you shall burn with fire.

**12:11** And thus shall you eat it: your loins girded, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand. And you shall eat it in haste -- it is a passover to YHWH. [fn8]

**12:12** And I will pass through the land of Egypt on that night, and I will strike every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from man to beast; and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments. I am YHWH. [fn9]

**12:13** And the blood shall be for you as a sign upon the houses where you are; and I will see the blood and I will pass over you, and there shall not be among you a plague of destruction when I strike in the land of Egypt. [fn10]

**12:14** And this day shall be for you a memorial, and you shall celebrate it as a feast to YHWH; throughout your generations, as a statute forever, you shall celebrate it.

---

## The Feast of Unleavened Bread (vv. 15-20)

**12:15** Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread. Indeed, on the first day you shall remove leaven from your houses, for whoever eats leavened bread from the first day until the seventh day -- that person shall be cut off from Israel. [fn11]

**12:16** And on the first day there shall be a holy convocation, and on the seventh day there shall be a holy convocation for you. No work shall be done on them; only what is eaten by every person -- that alone shall be prepared for you.

**12:17** And you shall keep the unleavened bread, for on this very day I brought out your hosts from the land of Egypt. And you shall keep this day throughout your generations as a statute forever. [fn12]

**12:18** In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, in the evening, you shall eat unleavened bread, until the twenty-first day of the month in the evening.

**12:19** For seven days, leaven shall not be found in your houses, for whoever eats what is leavened -- that person shall be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether sojourner or native of the land. [fn13]

**12:20** Anything leavened you shall not eat. In all your dwelling places you shall eat unleavened bread."

---

## Moses Instructs the Elders (vv. 21-28)

**12:21** And Moses called for all the elders of Israel and said to them, "Draw out and take for yourselves flock-animals according to your families, and slaughter the Passover. [fn14]

**12:22** And you shall take a bunch of hyssop and dip it in the blood that is in the basin, and you shall touch the lintel and the two doorposts with the blood that is in the basin. And you -- not a man of you shall go out from the door of his house until morning. [fn15]

**12:23** And YHWH will pass through to strike Egypt, and he will see the blood on the lintel and on the two doorposts, and YHWH will pass over the door, and he will not allow the destroyer to come into your houses to strike. [fn16]

**12:24** And you shall keep this thing as a statute for you and for your sons forever.

**12:25** And it shall be, when you come to the land which YHWH will give to you, as he has spoken, that you shall keep this service. [fn17]

**12:26** And it shall be, when your sons say to you, 'What is this service to you?'

**12:27** then you shall say, 'It is the sacrifice of the Passover to YHWH, who passed over the houses of the sons of Israel in Egypt when he struck Egypt, and our houses he delivered.'" And the people bowed down and worshiped. [fn18]

**12:28** And the sons of Israel went and did as YHWH had commanded Moses and Aaron; so they did.

---

## The Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn (vv. 29-36)

**12:29** And it was at midnight, and YHWH struck every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon, and every firstborn of the livestock. [fn19]

**12:30** And Pharaoh rose in the night -- he and all his servants and all Egypt -- and there was a great cry in Egypt, for there was not a house where there was not one dead. [fn20]

**12:31** And he called for Moses and Aaron at night and said, "Rise up, go out from the midst of my people, both you and the sons of Israel! And go, serve YHWH, as you have spoken! [fn21]

**12:32** Also your flocks, also your herds -- take them, as you have spoken, and go! And bless me also!" [fn22]

**12:33** And Egypt was urgent upon the people, to hasten to send them out from the land, for they said, "We are all dead men!" [fn23]

**12:34** And the people carried their dough before it was leavened, their kneading troughs bound up in their garments upon their shoulders.

**12:35** And the sons of Israel had done according to the word of Moses, and they had asked from Egypt articles of silver and articles of gold and garments.

**12:36** And YHWH had given the people favor in the eyes of Egypt, and they gave to them what they asked. And they plundered Egypt. [fn24]

---

## The Departure from Egypt (vv. 37-42)

**12:37** And the sons of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand men on foot, the men, besides the children. [fn25]

**12:38** And also a mixed multitude went up with them, and flocks and herds -- very much livestock. [fn26]

**12:39** And they baked the dough which they had brought out from Egypt into cakes of unleavened bread, for it had not leavened, because they were driven out from Egypt and could not delay, and also provisions they had not made for themselves.

**12:40** And the dwelling of the sons of Israel, which they had dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years. [fn27]

**12:41** And it was at the end of four hundred and thirty years, and it was on this very day, all the hosts of YHWH went out from the land of Egypt.

**12:42** It is a night of watching for YHWH, to bring them out from the land of Egypt. This is that night for YHWH -- a watching for all the sons of Israel throughout their generations. [fn28]

---

## Statutes of the Passover (vv. 43-51)

**12:43** And YHWH said to Moses and Aaron, "This is the statute of the Passover: No foreigner shall eat of it. [fn29]

**12:44** But every man's servant purchased with silver -- you shall circumcise him; then he shall eat of it.

**12:45** A temporary resident and a hired worker shall not eat of it. [fn30]

**12:46** In one house it shall be eaten. You shall not bring any of the flesh outside the house, and a bone you shall not break in it. [fn31]

**12:47** All the congregation of Israel shall keep it.

**12:48** And when a sojourner sojourns with you and would keep the Passover to YHWH, let every male of his be circumcised, and then he may draw near to keep it, and he shall be as a native of the land. But no uncircumcised person shall eat of it. [fn32]

**12:49** One law shall be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns in your midst." [fn33]

**12:50** And all the sons of Israel did as YHWH had commanded Moses and Aaron; so they did.

**12:51** **And it was on this very day that YHWH brought out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt by their hosts.**

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- YHWH speaks to Moses and Aaron together in Egypt** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon be'erets Mitsrayim lemor*. For the first time in the plague narrative, YHWH addresses both Moses and Aaron together with the geographic marker *be'erets Mitsrayim* ("in the land of Egypt"). This opens a new literary unit: the shift from plague announcement to covenant ritual institution. EMET transliterates all names (Mosheh, Aharon, Mitsrayim); LOGOS uses "the LORD" for YHWH; PNEUMA drops the conjunction "And" and the word "saying." The consensus follows SOPHIA convention: YHWH transliterated, standard English names (Moses, Aaron, Egypt), Hebrew conjunctions preserved ("And"), and "saying" retained as it reflects *lemor*. LXX: *eipen de kurios pros Mousēn kai Aaron en gē Aiguptō legōn*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Rosh chodashim*: "the head of months"** -- Hebrew: *hachodesh hazzeh lakhem rosh chodashim rishon hu lakhem lechodshei hashanah*. This verse institutes a calendar reset: the month of the Exodus (later Nisan/Aviv) becomes the *rosh* ("head") of all months. EMET renders "the head of months" -- preserving *rosh* as "head" with its connotations of authority and primacy. LOGOS renders "the beginning of months" -- following the LXX's *archē mēnōn*. PNEUMA renders freely: "your beginning -- the first of all your months, the starting point of your year." The consensus renders "the head of months" -- *rosh* means "head" (not merely "beginning"), and this word carries the weight of primacy and ruling authority. The month does not merely come first; it rules over all other months. The doubled *lakhem... lakhem* ("for you... for you") is preserved: this is Israel's calendar, not Egypt's.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Seh*: "a lamb" (covering both sheep and goats)** -- Hebrew: *veyiqchu lahem ish seh leveit-avot seh labbayit*. The noun *seh* is a generic term for a head of small livestock -- either a lamb (*keves*) or a kid (*ez*), as v. 5 clarifies with *min-hakkevashim umin-ha'izzim* ("from the sheep or from the goats"). EMET renders "a seh [lamb/kid]" with transliteration and gloss. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "a lamb." The consensus renders "a lamb" -- following the conventional English rendering while noting that the Hebrew *seh* encompasses both sheep and goats. The specification in v. 5 makes the semantic range explicit in the text itself. The LXX narrows to *probaton* ("sheep").

> **[fn4] v.5 -- *Tamim*: "unblemished"** -- Hebrew: *seh tamim zakhar ben-shanah*. The adjective *tamim* ("complete, whole, without defect") from *tamam* ("to be complete") specifies the ritual fitness of the animal. EMET transliterates: "seh tamim." LOGOS: "unblemished." PNEUMA: "without defect." The consensus follows LOGOS: "unblemished" -- a formal English equivalent that preserves the Hebrew's connotation of wholeness without paraphrase. The phrase *ben-shanah* ("son of a year") is rendered "a year old" for clarity.

> **[fn5] v.6 -- *Bein ha'arbayim*: "between the evenings"** -- Hebrew: *veshachatu oto kol qehal adat-Yisra'el bein ha'arbayim*. This temporal expression is one of the most debated in the Hebrew Bible. Literally "between the two evenings" (*arbayim* is a dual form of *erev*, "evening"), it has been interpreted as: (a) between the sun's decline and sunset (roughly 3:00-6:00 PM) -- the Pharisaic/rabbinic view that prevailed in Second Temple practice; (b) between sunset and full darkness (roughly 6:00-7:30 PM) -- the Samaritan/Sadducean view. EMET: "between the evenings [bein ha-arbayim]." LOGOS: "between the evenings [bein ha'arbayim]." PNEUMA: "at twilight." The consensus renders "between the evenings" -- preserving the Hebrew idiom and its inherent temporal ambiguity. PNEUMA's "at twilight" interprets the expression but collapses its dual structure. The LXX renders *pros esperan* ("toward evening"), simplifying the dual.

> **[fn6] v.7 -- *Mezuzot* and *mashqof*: "doorposts" and "lintel"** -- Hebrew: *velaqchu min-haddam venatnu al-shtei hamezuzot ve'al-hamashqof*. The *mezuzot* ("doorposts") are the vertical side-members of the doorframe; the *mashqof* ("lintel") is the horizontal beam above the door. All three translators agree on these renderings. Blood is applied to three points framing the entrance -- two sides and the top -- creating a portal of protection. The door-threshold itself is not marked; one walks through the blood without stepping on it.

> **[fn7] v.9 -- *Uvashel mevushal bamayim*: the cognate accusative** -- Hebrew: *al-tokhlu mimmennu na uvashel mevushal bamayim*. The emphatic construction *bashel mevushal* ("boiled, boiled") is a cognate accusative intensifying the prohibition. EMET: "boiled -- boiled in water." LOGOS: "boiled, boiled in water." PNEUMA: "boiled in water" (collapsing the doubling). The consensus preserves the doubled form: "boiled -- boiled in water" -- maintaining the Hebrew's emphatic prohibition pattern, consistent with SOPHIA's convention of preserving intensification structures.

> **[fn8] v.11 -- *Bechipazon* and *pesach hu laYHWH*: "in haste" and "it is a passover to YHWH"** -- Hebrew: *va'akhaltem oto bechipazon pesach hu laYHWH*. The noun *chipazon* ("haste, trepidation") occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible (here, Deut. 16:3, Isa. 52:12). It conveys not merely speed but urgent, anxious haste -- the haste of those who must flee before a window closes. EMET: "in chipazon [haste]." LOGOS: "in haste." PNEUMA: "in urgent haste." The consensus renders "in haste" -- the simplest formal equivalent, following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "urgent" adds a modifier not in the Hebrew (the urgency is already embedded in *chipazon* itself). The declaration *pesach hu laYHWH* names the meal and festival for the first time. EMET: "pesach to YHWH." LOGOS: "a Passover to the LORD." PNEUMA: "the Passover of YHWH." The consensus renders "a passover to YHWH" -- the indefinite article reflects the Hebrew's lack of definite article on *pesach*, and *laYHWH* is "to YHWH" (dative), not "of YHWH" (genitive).

> **[fn9] v.12 -- *Uvekhol-elohei Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim*: "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments"** -- Hebrew: *uvekhol-elohei Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim ani YHWH*. This clause makes explicit what has been implicit throughout the plague narrative: the plagues are judgments against the Egyptian gods themselves. The noun *shefatim* ("judgments, acts of judicial sentence") from *shafat* ("to judge") is legal terminology -- these are verdicts, not random acts. All three translators agree on the core rendering. The LXX renders *ekdikēsis* ("vengeance") rather than the more judicial "judgments" -- the consensus follows the MT's legal nuance. The clause *ani YHWH* ("I am YHWH") functions as a divine signature authenticating the verdict.

> **[fn10] v.13 -- *Ufasachti*: "I will pass over" -- the defining verb of the Passover** -- Hebrew: *vera'iti et-haddam ufasachti aleikhem velo-yihyeh vakhem negef lemashchit*. The verb *ufasachti* from *pasach* is the verb that gives the festival its name. Its semantic range includes: (a) "to pass over, skip" -- YHWH skips the marked houses; (b) "to protect, hover over" -- from an Akkadian cognate, YHWH stands guard at the door (cf. Isa. 31:5); (c) "to limp, skip" (cf. 1 Kings 18:21). The protective sense is supported by v. 23, where YHWH stations himself at the doorway to prevent the destroyer from entering, and by the LXX's *skepasō humas* ("I will shelter you") at v. 13. EMET: "I will pasakh [pass over/protect]." LOGOS: "I will pass over." PNEUMA: "I will pass over." The consensus renders "I will pass over" -- the traditional rendering that preserves the etymological connection to "Passover" (*pesach*), while the protective dimension is made explicit in v. 23. The phrase *negef lemashchit* is rendered "a plague of destruction" (LOGOS) rather than "a plague for destruction" (EMET) or "no plague of destruction will touch you" (PNEUMA, restructured). The word *mashchit* ("destroyer") from *shachat* ("to destroy, corrupt") introduces the destroying agent that v. 23 will distinguish from YHWH.

> **[fn11] v.15 -- *Se'or*, *chamets*, and *karet*: leaven terminology and the penalty of excision** -- Three distinct Hebrew terms for leaven/leavened appear in this section: (a) *se'or* ("leaven, sourdough starter") -- the fermenting agent itself; (b) *chamets* ("leavened bread") -- bread that has undergone fermentation; (c) *machmetset* (v. 19, "what is leavened") -- a Hiphil participial form, anything caused to become leavened. The penalty for eating *chamets* during the festival is *karet* -- being "cut off from Israel" -- the most severe communal sanction, elevating the unleavened bread requirement from culinary preference to covenant obligation. All three translators render these terms consistently. EMET transliterates with glosses; LOGOS and PNEUMA translate directly. The consensus uses English equivalents: "leaven" (*se'or*), "leavened bread" (*chamets*), "what is leavened" (*machmetset*).

> **[fn12] v.17 -- *Be'etsem hayyom hazzeh*: "on this very day"** -- Hebrew: *ki be'etsem hayyom hazzeh hotseiti et-tsiv'oteikhem me'erets Mitsrayim*. The phrase *be'etsem hayyom hazzeh* uses *etsem* ("bone, substance, self") -- the day is as substantial and specific as a bone. This emphatic temporal marker recurs at vv. 41 and 51, creating a three-fold structural bracket. The word *tsiv'oteikhem* ("your hosts") from *tsava* ("army, host") describes Israel departing not as a rabble of slaves but as organized hosts -- a military metaphor for the redeemed people. All three translators agree on "on this very day." The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

> **[fn13] v.19 -- *Ger* and *ezrach ha'arets*: "sojourner" and "native of the land"** -- Hebrew: *baggeir uve'ezrach ha'arets*. The *ger* ("sojourner, resident alien") is one who lives among Israel without being born into it. The *ezrach* ("native, native-born") from *zarach* ("to rise, shine") is the indigenous Israelite. The *karet* penalty applies equally to both -- the first statement of legal equality between native and sojourner in Torah, anticipating v. 49's declaration of *torah achat*. EMET transliterates both terms. LOGOS: "sojourner" and "native of the land." PNEUMA: "foreigner" and "native." The consensus follows LOGOS: "sojourner" for *ger* (not "foreigner," which confuses *ger* with *ben-nekhar*) and "native of the land" for *ezrach ha'arets*.

> **[fn14] v.21 -- *Mishkhu*: "draw out"** -- Hebrew: *mishkhu uqchu lakhem tso'n lemishpechoteikhem veshachatu happasach*. The verb *mishkhu* from *mashakh* ("to draw, pull") may imply drawing the animal from the flock, or -- as some interpreters suggest -- withdrawing from Egyptian idolatry, since the lamb was sacred to Egyptian religion and slaughtering it was an act of separation. EMET: "draw out and take for yourselves tso'n [flock-animals]." LOGOS: "draw out and take for yourselves lambs." PNEUMA: "go at once. Select lambs." The consensus renders "draw out and take for yourselves flock-animals" -- preserving the verb *mishkhu* as "draw out" (EMET/LOGOS) and rendering *tso'n* as "flock-animals" rather than "lambs" (since *tso'n* is the general term for small livestock, not specifically lambs).

> **[fn15] v.22 -- *Saf*: "basin" (with threshold ambiguity) and *ezov*: "hyssop"** -- Hebrew: *ulqachtem agudat ezov utvaltem baddam asher bassaf*. The noun *saf* has a dual meaning: "basin" (a vessel for collecting blood) or "threshold" (the bottom of the doorframe). The ambiguity may be intentional: the blood is collected at the threshold-basin, and from this liminal point the hyssop transfers it to the doorframe. EMET: "basin [ba-saf]" with note on dual meaning. LOGOS: "basin." PNEUMA: "basin." The consensus renders "basin" as the primary meaning, following all three translators, while noting the threshold resonance. The *ezov* ("hyssop") is a small aromatic plant (probably Syrian oregano, *Origanum syriacum*) used throughout the Torah for ritual purification (Lev. 14:4-6, Num. 19:6, Ps. 51:7). The verb *vehigga'tem* ("and you shall touch/strike") from *naga* ("to touch") -- EMET: "touch"; LOGOS: "touch"; PNEUMA: "strike." The consensus follows "touch" -- the verb *naga* means "to touch, to reach" and does not imply forceful striking.

> **[fn16] v.23 -- *Ufasach YHWH al-happetach* and *hammashchit*: YHWH, the Passover, and the destroyer** -- Hebrew: *ve'avar YHWH linggof et-Mitsrayim... ufasach YHWH al-happetach velo yitten hammashchit lavo el-batteikhem linggof*. This is the theological crux of the chapter. Two distinct agents are named: (a) YHWH, who "passes through" (*avar*) to strike Egypt, then "passes over" (*pasach*) the door; (b) *hammashchit* ("the destroyer"), whom YHWH "will not allow" (*lo yitten*, "will not give/permit") to enter the houses. The verb *pasach* here takes YHWH as subject and *al-happetach* ("over the doorway") as complement -- God personally stations himself over each marked doorway as a protective presence. The *mashchit* is an agent distinct from YHWH -- a destroying force or destroying angel that YHWH restrains. All three translators agree on "the destroyer" for *hammashchit* and "pass over" for *pasach*. PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion: "will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses and strike you down." The consensus follows the formal rendering: "he will not allow the destroyer to come into your houses to strike" -- preserving the infinitive *linggof* ("to strike") without adding an object not in the Hebrew. The LXX reads *ton olothreuonta* ("the destroying one"), using a participle that became the standard term for the destroying angel in later Jewish and Christian tradition (cf. 1 Cor. 10:10).

> **[fn17] v.25 -- *Avodah*: "service"** -- Hebrew: *ushemartem et-ha'avodah hazzot*. The word *avodah* means both "service/worship" and "labor" -- the same root (*avad*) that described Israel's slave-labor in Egypt (1:14, 2:23, 5:9). The ritual transforms *avodah*-as-slavery into *avodah*-as-worship. EMET: "avodah [service/worship]." LOGOS: "service." PNEUMA: "practice." The consensus renders "service" (LOGOS) -- preserving the ambiguity between worship and labor that the Hebrew *avodah* carries. PNEUMA's "practice" loses the theological resonance entirely.

> **[fn18] v.27 -- *Zevach-pesach hu laYHWH*: "It is the sacrifice of the Passover to YHWH"** -- Hebrew: *va'amartem zevach-pesach hu laYHWH asher pasach al-battei venei-Yisra'el beMitsrayim*. The *zevach* ("sacrifice," from *zavach*, "to slaughter") identifies the Passover as a sacrificial offering, not merely a meal. The verb *pasach* recurs as a verbal explanation of the noun *pesach*: the Passover is so called because YHWH "passed over" the houses of Israel. EMET: "a zevach-pesach [sacrifice of passover] to YHWH, who pasakh [passed over]." LOGOS: "the sacrifice of the Passover to the LORD, who passed over." PNEUMA: "the Passover sacrifice to YHWH, because he passed over." The consensus renders "the sacrifice of the Passover to YHWH, who passed over" -- preserving the formal construct *zevach-pesach* in English as "the sacrifice of the Passover" and the relative clause *asher pasach* as "who passed over." EMET's response includes both *vayiqqod* ("bowed the head") and *vayishtakhavu* ("prostrated themselves") -- a double gesture. LOGOS: "bowed down and worshiped." PNEUMA: "bowed their heads and worshipped." The consensus renders "bowed down and worshiped" (LOGOS), combining both gestures into natural English.

> **[fn19] v.29 -- *Bachatsi hallaylah*: "at midnight" (precise execution)** -- Hebrew: *vayhi bachatsi hallaylah vaYHWH hikkah khol-bekhor*. Note the shift from 11:4 *kachatsot hallaylah* ("about midnight," with the approximating preposition *ke-*) to *bachatsi hallaylah* ("at midnight," with the locative preposition *be-*). The announcement was approximate; the execution is precise. All three translators render "at midnight." The social spectrum also shifts: in 11:5, the lowest class was "the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones"; here it is "the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon" (*bekhor hashebi asher beveit habor*). The *shevi* ("captive, prisoner") is someone even lower than the slave -- locked in a pit, forgotten. EMET: "the captive who was in the house of the pit." LOGOS: "the captive who was in the dungeon." PNEUMA: "the prisoner in the dungeon." The consensus renders "the captive who was in the dungeon" -- *shevi* is "captive" (more precise than "prisoner"), and *beit habor* ("house of the pit") is rendered "dungeon" for clarity.

> **[fn20] v.30 -- *Tse'aqah gedolah*: "a great cry" -- fulfillment of 11:6** -- The *tse'aqah gedolah* predicted in 11:6 now erupts. The double negative *ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met* ("for there was not a house where there was not one dead") is totalizing. All three translators agree on the rendering. The word *tse'aqah* is the same used for Israel's cry under slavery (2:23, 3:7) -- the oppressor's cry now mirrors the cry of the oppressed.

> **[fn21] v.31 -- Pharaoh's capitulation: rapid-fire imperatives** -- Hebrew: *qumu tse'u mittokh ammi... ulkhu ivdu et-YHWH kedabberkhem*. Four imperatives in quick succession: *qumu* ("rise!"), *tse'u* ("go out!"), *lekhu* ("go!"), *ivdu* ("serve!"). The phrase *kedabberkhem* ("as you have spoken") repeated in v. 32 constitutes unconditional surrender -- everything Moses demanded, Pharaoh now grants. EMET preserves the Hebrew syntax most closely. LOGOS follows formal equivalence. PNEUMA restructures: "Rise! Go out from among my people." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "Rise up, go out from the midst of my people" -- preserving *mittokh ammi* ("from the midst of my people") rather than the looser "from among" (PNEUMA).

> **[fn22] v.32 -- *Uverakhetem gam-oti*: "And bless me also!"** -- Hebrew: *uverakhetem gam-oti*. Pharaoh's final words to Moses are a plea for blessing. The verb *barekh* (Piel of *barakh*) means "to bless" -- to invoke divine favor. The man who declared "Who is YHWH that I should listen to his voice?" (5:2) now asks for YHWH's blessing through Moses. The particle *gam* ("also") is poignant -- bless me too, along with yourselves. All three translators agree on the rendering. The consensus follows the unanimous reading: "And bless me also!"

> **[fn23] v.33 -- *Vateckezaq Mitsrayim al-ha'am*: "Egypt was urgent upon the people"** -- Hebrew: *vateckezaq Mitsrayim al-ha'am lemaher leshalkham*. The verb *vateckezaq* from *chazaq* ("to be strong, urgent") is the same root used for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart throughout the plague narrative. Now the urgency (*chazaq*) that held Israel captive drives them free -- a deliberate irony. EMET: "Mitsrayim was urgent upon the people." LOGOS: "Egypt was urgent upon the people." PNEUMA: "The Egyptians pressed the people urgently." The consensus follows LOGOS: "Egypt was urgent upon the people" -- preserving the Hebrew's syntax where Egypt (the nation as a collective) is the subject, rather than "the Egyptians" (PNEUMA). The phrase *kullanu metim* ("we are all dead men") is rendered literally by all three translators.

> **[fn24] v.36 -- *Vaynatslu et-Mitsrayim*: "they plundered Egypt"** -- Hebrew: *vaynatslu et-Mitsrayim*. The verb *vaynatslu* (Piel of *natsal*) means "to strip, plunder, despoil." In its Hiphil form (*hitstsil*), this root means "to deliver, rescue" (cf. v. 27). The deliberate wordplay: the delivered (*natsal* Hiphil) despoil (*natsal* Piel). All three translators agree on "plundered" or equivalent. The consensus follows the unanimous "plundered."

> **[fn25] v.37 -- *Keshesh-me'ot elef ragli*: "about six hundred thousand men on foot"** -- Hebrew: *keshesh-me'ot elef ragli haggevarim levad mittaf*. The number *shesh-me'ot elef* ("six hundred thousand") has been extensively debated. The Hebrew word *elef* can mean "thousand" or "clan/military unit" -- some scholars propose reading "six hundred clans" rather than "six hundred thousand individuals," which would yield a much smaller total population. Taken at face value, 600,000 adult males plus women and children would imply 2-3 million people. EMET: "about six hundred thousand ragli [on foot], the men [ha-gevarim], besides the taf [little-ones]." LOGOS: "about six hundred thousand men on foot, the men, besides the children." PNEUMA: "about six hundred thousand men on foot, not counting women and children." The consensus follows the MT at face value: "about six hundred thousand men on foot, the men, besides the children" -- rendering *ragli* as "on foot" (pedestrian, infantry-like departure), *haggevarim* as "the men" (adult males), and *taf* as "children" (dependents). PNEUMA's addition of "women and" is interpretively reasonable but not in the Hebrew. The prefix *ke-* ("about") preserves the approximation.

> **[fn26] v.38 -- *Erev rav*: "a mixed multitude"** -- Hebrew: *vegam-erev rav alah ittam*. The phrase *erev rav* ("a great mixed multitude") refers to non-Israelites who joined the Exodus. The noun *erev* (from the root *'-r-v*, "to mix, to mingle") denotes a heterogeneous group -- possibly Egyptians, other Semitic peoples, mixed families, fellow slaves of other ethnicities. EMET: "an erev rav [mixed multitude / great mixture]." LOGOS: "a mixed multitude." PNEUMA: "a large mixed crowd." The consensus renders "a mixed multitude" -- following LOGOS and the traditional English rendering. The Hebrew *rav* modifies the *erev*: a great mixing, an extensive mixture. PNEUMA's "crowd" flattens the Hebrew's emphasis on ethnic/social mixing.

> **[fn27] v.40 -- The 430 years: MT vs. LXX variant** -- Hebrew (MT): *umoshav benei Yisra'el asher yashvu beMitsrayim sheloshim shanah ve'arba me'ot shanah*. The MT states that the dwelling of the sons of Israel in Egypt was 430 years. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads: *hē de katoikēsis tōn huiōn Israēl hēn katōkēsan en gē Aiguptō kai en gē Chanaan etē tetrakosia triakonta* -- adding "and in the land of Canaan," distributing the 430 years across both the patriarchal sojourn in Canaan and the Egyptian bondage. This LXX reading is supported by the Samaritan Pentateuch and was known to Paul (Gal. 3:17). If the 430 years covers both Canaan and Egypt, the actual time in Egypt would be approximately 215 years. The consensus follows the MT reading, noting the LXX variant as one of the most significant chronological divergences between the Hebrew and Greek traditions.

> **[fn28] v.42 -- *Leil shimmurim*: "a night of watching"** -- Hebrew: *leil shimmurim hu laYHWH lehotsi'am me'erets Mitsrayim hu-hallaylah hazzeh laYHWH shimmurim lekhol-benei Yisra'el ledorotam*. The noun *shimmurim* is an intensive plural of *shimmur* ("watching, keeping, vigil"), from *shamar* ("to guard, to watch over"). This word occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible -- a hapax legomenon. The phrase creates a mutual vigil: YHWH watched for Israel ("a night of watching for YHWH, to bring them out"), and Israel watches for YHWH ("a watching for all the sons of Israel throughout their generations"). EMET: "a leil shimmurim [night of watchings/vigils]." LOGOS: "a night of watching." PNEUMA: "a night of watchful vigil." The consensus renders "a night of watching" (LOGOS) -- the simplest English equivalent that preserves the Hebrew's guard-keeping sense. PNEUMA's "watchful vigil" is tautological ("watchful" + "vigil" both derive from the same semantic field). The doubled structure of the verse is preserved in the translation: "It is a night of watching for YHWH... a watching for all the sons of Israel."

> **[fn29] v.43 -- *Ben-nekhar*: "foreigner" (distinct from *ger*)** -- Hebrew: *kol-ben-nekhar lo-yokhal bo*. The *ben-nekhar* ("son of a foreigner/foreign-ness") is categorically distinct from the *ger* ("sojourner") of v. 48. The *ben-nekhar* has no covenant attachment to Israel; the *ger* has taken up residence within Israel's community. The Passover statutes (vv. 43-49) establish a careful hierarchy of inclusion: foreigners are excluded (v. 43); purchased servants may participate after circumcision (v. 44); temporary residents and hired workers are excluded (v. 45); sojourners may participate after circumcision and become "as a native" (v. 48). The boundary marker is circumcision, not ethnicity. All three translators agree on "foreigner" for *ben-nekhar*.

> **[fn30] v.45 -- *Toshav* and *sakhir*: "temporary resident" and "hired worker"** -- Hebrew: *toshav vesakhir lo-yokhal bo*. The *toshav* ("temporary resident/dweller") from *yashav* ("to dwell") is distinct from the *ger*: the *toshav* is a sojourner with a less permanent attachment. The *sakhir* ("hired worker") from *sakhar* ("to hire") is a wage-laborer. Neither has entered the covenant through circumcision. EMET: "toshav [resident] and sakhir [hired worker]." LOGOS: "a sojourner and a hired worker." PNEUMA: "a temporary resident or hired worker." The consensus renders "a temporary resident and a hired worker" -- combining PNEUMA's clarifying "temporary" for *toshav* with LOGOS/EMET's "and" (the Hebrew uses *ve-*, "and," not "or").

> **[fn31] v.46 -- *Ve'etsem lo tishberu-vo*: "a bone you shall not break in it"** -- Hebrew: *ve'etsem lo tishberu-vo*. The prohibition against breaking a bone preserves the wholeness of the sacrifice. The *etsem* ("bone") is the same word used in the phrase *be'etsem hayyom hazzeh* ("on this very day," vv. 17, 41, 51) -- a verbal echo linking the substance of the day to the substance of the sacrifice. Three regulations for the Passover lamb: (a) eaten in one house, (b) no flesh taken outside, (c) no bone broken. All three translators agree on the rendering. The unbroken skeleton represents the integrity of the sacrifice -- the Passover lamb is offered whole to God. The LXX renders *ostoun ou suntripsete ap' autou* ("a bone you shall not crush from it"), using the verb *suntribō* that is cited in John 19:36.

> **[fn32] v.48 -- The *ger* who circumcises: "he shall be as a native of the land"** -- Hebrew: *veki-yagur ittekha ger ve'asah fesach laYHWH himmol lo khol-zakhar ve'az yiqrav la'asoto vehayah ke'ezrach ha'arets*. The *ger* who circumcises all his males may participate fully in the Passover and becomes *ke'ezrach ha'arets* ("as a native of the land"). The verb *yiqrav* ("he may draw near") from *qarav* is a cultic term for approaching sacred things. Circumcision -- not ethnicity -- is the covenant boundary. EMET: "he shall be as a native of the land [ke-ezrach ha-arets]." LOGOS: "he shall be as a native of the land." PNEUMA: "he will be treated as a native of the land." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "he shall be as a native of the land" -- preserving the Hebrew's direct statement of equivalence rather than PNEUMA's passive construction ("will be treated as").

> **[fn33] v.49 -- *Torah achat*: "one law" -- legal equality for native and sojourner** -- Hebrew: *torah achat yihyeh la'ezrach velaggeir haggar betokhekhem*. The phrase *torah achat* ("one law, a single instruction") establishes legal equality between the native Israelite (*ezrach*) and the sojourner (*ger*). This principle recurs throughout the Pentateuch (Lev. 24:22, Num. 9:14, 15:15-16, 29) and is foundational for Israel's jurisprudence. All three translators agree on the core rendering. EMET: "Torah achat [one torah/law]." LOGOS: "One law." PNEUMA: "The same law applies." The consensus renders "One law shall be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns in your midst" -- preserving the formal Hebrew structure. PNEUMA's "the same law applies" is a paraphrase that loses the emphatic *achat* ("one") and the Hebrew's declarative syntax.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. *Rosh Chodashim* (v. 2) -- "Head of Months" vs. "Beginning of Months"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the head of months [rosh chodashim]" | Literal: preserves *rosh* as "head" |
| LOGOS | "the beginning of months [rosh chodashim]" | Formal: follows LXX *archē* |
| PNEUMA | "your beginning -- the first of all your months" | Dynamic: free paraphrase |

The consensus renders "the head of months." The Hebrew *rosh* means "head" -- not merely "beginning" (*reshit* or *techillah* would be the expected words for "beginning"). The word *rosh* carries connotations of authority, primacy, and rule: this month is the ruling month, the chief month. LOGOS's "beginning" follows the LXX's *archē mēnōn* but flattens the Hebrew's more muscular image. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses both the Hebrew idiom and the word order.

### 2. *Seh* (v. 3) -- "Lamb" as Convention, Noting Broader Range

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "seh [lamb/kid]" | Literal: transliteration with gloss |
| LOGOS | "lamb [seh]" | Formal: conventional, with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "lamb" | Dynamic: conventional |

All three effectively render "lamb." The consensus follows "lamb" as the standard English convention, noting that *seh* encompasses both sheep and goats. The text itself resolves the ambiguity in v. 5 ("from the sheep or from the goats"), making a footnote-level clarification in v. 3 sufficient.

### 3. *Bein Ha'arbayim* (v. 6) -- "Between the Evenings" vs. "At Twilight"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "between the evenings [bein ha-arbayim]" | Literal: preserves idiom |
| LOGOS | "between the evenings [bein ha'arbayim]" | Formal: preserves idiom |
| PNEUMA | "at twilight" | Dynamic: interprets the idiom |

The consensus preserves "between the evenings" -- the Hebrew idiom with its dual structure and inherent temporal ambiguity. PNEUMA's "at twilight" is a reasonable interpretation but collapses the dual form and eliminates the ambiguity that has generated centuries of interpretive discussion.

### 4. *Pesach hu laYHWH* (v. 11) -- "A Passover to YHWH" vs. "The Passover of YHWH"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pesach to YHWH" | Literal: no article, dative |
| LOGOS | "a Passover to the LORD" | Formal: indefinite article, dative |
| PNEUMA | "the Passover of YHWH" | Dynamic: definite article, genitive |

The consensus renders "a passover to YHWH." The Hebrew has no definite article on *pesach* (this is the first naming of the festival), so the indefinite article is appropriate. The preposition *laYHWH* is dative ("to/for YHWH"), not genitive ("of YHWH"). PNEUMA's "the Passover of YHWH" introduces both a definite article and a genitive construction not present in the Hebrew.

### 5. *Pasach* (v. 13) -- "Pass Over" vs. "Protect"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pasakh [pass over/protect]" | Literal: dual rendering with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "pass over" | Formal: traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "pass over" | Dynamic: traditional rendering |

All three default to "pass over." The consensus follows this unanimous rendering while noting that the protective sense is strongly supported by v. 23 (where YHWH stations himself at the door) and the LXX's *skepasō* ("I will shelter") at v. 13. The traditional rendering "pass over" preserves the etymological connection between the verb *pasach* and the noun *pesach* ("Passover") that is essential for English readers.

### 6. *Saf* (v. 22) -- "Basin" vs. "Threshold"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "basin [ba-saf]" with threshold note | Literal: primary meaning with note |
| LOGOS | "basin [saf]" with threshold note | Formal: primary meaning with note |
| PNEUMA | "basin" | Dynamic: primary meaning |

The consensus renders "basin" following all three translators, noting the threshold ambiguity. The LXX interprets *saf* as the door-area (*tou haimatos tou para tēn thuran*, "the blood beside the door") rather than as a vessel, supporting the threshold reading. The ambiguity may be intentional -- the blood collected at the threshold, in a vessel at the foot of the door.

### 7. *Hammashchit* (v. 23) -- "The Destroyer": Identity and Agency

All three translators render *hammashchit* as "the destroyer." The theological question is who or what the *mashchit* is -- an angel, a demonic force YHWH restrains, a personification of plague, or a hypostatic attribute of divine wrath. Verse 23 distinguishes YHWH (who "passes through" and "passes over") from the *mashchit* (whom YHWH "will not allow to enter"). This distinction suggests the *mashchit* is an agent under YHWH's authority -- it goes where YHWH permits and is restrained where YHWH forbids. Later tradition develops the concept of a destroying angel (*mal'akh hamashchit*, 2 Sam. 24:16, 1 Chr. 21:15). The consensus preserves the ambiguity by rendering simply "the destroyer" without further specification.

### 8. *Zevach-Pesach* (v. 27) -- The Ritual Name

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "zevach-pesach [sacrifice of passover]" | Literal: transliteration with gloss |
| LOGOS | "the sacrifice of the Passover [zevach-pesach]" | Formal: English with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "the Passover sacrifice" | Dynamic: reversed word order |

The consensus renders "the sacrifice of the Passover" (LOGOS) -- preserving the Hebrew construct chain order (*zevach-pesach* = "sacrifice of the Passover") rather than reversing it to "the Passover sacrifice" (PNEUMA). The word *zevach* identifies the Passover as a *sacrifice* -- a slaughtered offering to God, not merely a commemorative meal.

### 9. *Elef* (v. 37) -- "Thousand" vs. "Clan"

The consensus follows the MT at face value: "about six hundred thousand." The Hebrew *elef* can mean either "thousand" or "clan/military unit" (cf. Judg. 6:15, 1 Sam. 10:19). If "clan," the number would be vastly smaller. All three translators render the number conventionally. The LXX's *eis hexakosias chiliadas* ("about six hundred thousands") confirms the traditional numerical reading. The consensus notes the alternative interpretation without adopting it.

### 10. *Erev Rav* (v. 38) -- "A Mixed Multitude"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "erev rav [mixed multitude / great mixture]" | Literal: transliteration with dual gloss |
| LOGOS | "a mixed multitude [erev rav]" | Formal: traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a large mixed crowd" | Dynamic: interpretive |

The consensus renders "a mixed multitude" -- the traditional English rendering that preserves the Hebrew's emphasis on ethnic/social mixing (*erev* from *'-r-v*, "to mix"). PNEUMA's "crowd" loses the mixing imagery.

### 11. *Leil Shimmurim* (v. 42) -- "A Night of Watching"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "leil shimmurim [night of watchings/vigils]" | Literal: transliteration |
| LOGOS | "a night of watching [leil shimmurim]" | Formal: English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a night of watchful vigil" | Dynamic: intensified |

The consensus renders "a night of watching" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "watchful vigil" is tautological. EMET's "watchings" (plural) captures the intensive plural *shimmurim* but is awkward in English. "A night of watching" conveys the guard-keeping sense of *shamar* while remaining natural.

### 12. *Torah Achat* (v. 49) -- "One Law"

All three translators agree on the substance: one law for native and sojourner. The disagreement is structural. EMET transliterates: "Torah achat [one torah/law]." LOGOS renders formally: "One law." PNEUMA paraphrases: "The same law applies." The consensus follows LOGOS: "One law shall be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns in your midst" -- preserving the emphatic *achat* ("one") and the declarative Hebrew syntax.

### 13. Recurring Convention Disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (He, His, My); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Aharon, Mitsrayim); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Aaron, Egypt). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**e. Transliteration in-text:** EMET embeds Hebrew transliterations throughout the verse text; LOGOS places transliterations in brackets after English; PNEUMA uses English only. SOPHIA follows English text with Hebrew noted in footnotes. (Recurring, LOW)

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 12

*Note: Exodus 12 contains no hardening-verb instances. The last hardening formula was 11:10 ("YHWH strengthened [chazaq, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh"). The next hardening will occur in 14:4 ("I will strengthen the heart of Pharaoh"), when Pharaoh pursues the departed Israelites to the Sea of Reeds. The entire Passover-departure narrative of chapters 12-13 is thus bracketed by divine hardenings -- the hardening that prevented release (11:10) and the hardening that drives pursuit (14:4). In between, the Passover narrative unfolds without any mention of Pharaoh's heart.*

*However, note the ironic use of chazaq in v. 33: vateckezaq Mitsrayim al-ha'am ("Egypt was urgent upon the people") -- the same root that described Pharaoh's hardened resistance now describes Egypt's desperate urgency to expel Israel. The verb that held Israel captive now drives them free.*

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 12:1-51 (51 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant (especially the 430-year chronological divergence at v. 40, the protective skepasō for pasach at v. 13, and the threshold/basin ambiguity of saf at v. 22). No hardening verbs in chapter 12; ironic chazaq noted at v. 33. Key renderings: "the head of months" (v. 2, preserving rosh), "between the evenings" (v. 6, preserving temporal ambiguity), "in haste" (v. 11, chipazon), "a passover to YHWH" (v. 11, first naming), "I will pass over" (v. 13, pasach), "the destroyer" (v. 23, mashchit), "the sacrifice of the Passover" (v. 27, zevach-pesach), "at midnight" (v. 29, precise execution), "a mixed multitude" (v. 38, erev rav), "a night of watching" (v. 42, leil shimmurim), "a bone you shall not break in it" (v. 46), "one law" (v. 49, torah achat). The 18 key translation decisions identified in the prompt have been adjudicated with full documentation.*

--- SOPHIA
